text: Long before Csikszentmihalyi named it, William James described a related phenomenon in his 1890 Principles of Psychology: the experience of being so absorbed in thought that the room disappears. James called this the "stream of consciousness" — not the literary technique it later became, but a description of attention as something that flows, narrows, and carries us. He noted that voluntary attention is exhausting and brief, while involuntary attention — captured by interest — is self-sustaining. Flow can be read as the condition in which voluntary and involuntary attention merge: we choose to engage, but engagement then carries us forward without effort. James called this the "willing of attention" becoming "a rolling snowball."
question: How does the passage characterize the relationship between voluntary and involuntary attention in flow?

options.text: Voluntary attention replaces involuntary attention as skill increases
They merge, so that chosen engagement then carries forward without effort
Involuntary attention suppresses voluntary attention through habit formation
Both forms of attention are suspended during genuine flow states
